title: practical english usage - Part 40
content:
and giving instructions.

Occasionally the simple present is used with a future meaning when asking for 3  instructions:        Where       do   I pay?

Alex will see us tomorrow if he has time, (     .. .ff-he-wiU-have-fime:) not She'll pay us back when she gets a job. (     .. .-when-she^U-get-afobr) not I'll tell you what I find out. (     .. .-what I'll find out-.) for example after what, where, when, until, if, than. For details, >231.

The  simple present is often used with a future meaning in subordinate clauses - 2 subordinate         clauses:     when      she    gets     a  job The summer  term will start on April 10th.

Will is also usually possible in these cases.

The sun rises at 6.13 tomorrow.

My plane leaves at three o'clock.

What time does the bus arrive in Seattle?

The summer  term starts on April 10th.

regular schedule or something similar.

common when we  are talking about events which  are part of a timetable, a We   can sometimes use the simple present to talk about the future. This is 1  timetables,        etc:  The     summer         term      starts      ...

37 simple     present      for   future For a comparison with will, ►    39.

I'm not washing your socks - forget it!

I'm sorry - you're not taking my car.

The  present progressive is common in emphatic refusals.

You're not wearing that skirt to school.

She's taking that medicine whether she likes it or not!

things.

The  present progressive can be used to insist that people do things or do not do 3  commands        and   refusals grammar •  38   will For other uses of will, ►    69.5. 73 , 79-80.

For exceptions, ►    231.4, 243.

I'll phone you when I arrive. (     .. . when I will arrive.) In subordinate clauses, we  usually use present tenses instead of will (►   231.2).

5  not   used    in  subordinate         clauses:     when      I arrive For details, ►    35-36.

I'm seeing the headmaster on Monday. My sister's going to have a baby.

often prefer a present form (usually present progressive or going to ...).

When  future events are already decided, or when we  can 'see them coming’ , we 4 future     events     already      decided:     will    not   used Don't leave me. I'll cry!

'Come out for a drink.' 'No, I'll miss the film on TV if I do.' Look out - you'll fall! (If you're not more careful.) If the weather's fine, we'll have the party in the garden.

He'll have an accident if he goes on driving like that.

something else happens.

Will is often used to express conditional ideas, when we say what will happen if 3 conditional        use:  You'll     fall    if  you're      not    careful.

Who do you think will win on Saturday? You'll never finish that book.

Tomorrow will be warm, with some cloud in the afternoon.

guess or calculate will happen.

We often use will in predictions of future events - to talk about what we  think, contact, causing the explosive to detonate.

In another thirteen minutes the alarm will go off. This will close an electrical Karen will start work some time next week.

Will all the family be at the wedding?

It'll be spring soon.

Will + infinitive is used to give (or ask for) information about the future.

2 use:  giving     information         about     the   future;     predicting Contractions: I'll, you'll, etc; won't /wə   unt/ ►    80.2.

almost non-existent in American English. For situations where it is still used, difference of meaning in most situations. However, shall is now rare, and it is Some British people use I shall and we shall instead of I/we will, with no It will be cold tomorrow. Where will you spend the night?

will + infinitive without to 1  forms 38 will Section Talking     about     the   Future          4 grammar •  39   will, going to and present progressive: advanced points (speaker’ s knowledge about genetics) The baby will certainly have blue eyes, because both parents have.

- Alice is going to have a baby, (outside evidence - she is pregnant now) 1 reckon it'll cost about £3,000  to put in new lights, (the speaker's opinion) (outside evidence - the builder’ s letter) - I've just heard from the builder. That roof repair's going to cost £7,000.
